name: Yaoguai
description: Yaoguai (妖怪), often translated as 'demon' or 'monster,' are creatures in Chinese mythology that have attained supernatural powers through the cultivation of spiritual energy, often over many years. Unlike gods or immortals who are typically born with or granted divine status, Yaoguai are usually animals, plants, or even inanimate objects that have gained sentience and magical abilities. Their motivations and alignment can vary wildly, ranging from mischievous tricksters to benevolent protectors, to malevolent destroyers of humanity. They are often depicted as shapeshifters, able to assume human or animal forms, and possess a wide array of magical abilities depending on their nature and the length of their cultivation. Many popular stories in Chinese folklore feature Yaoguai as antagonists or protagonists, testing the virtues of mortals or challenging the established order. They embody the untamed potential of the natural world and the possibility of transformation through dedicated practice, be it for good or ill. The term 'Yaoguai' is broad, encompassing a vast bestiary of supernatural beings, each with its own unique characteristics and stories.
subtitle: Demons and Monsters of Chinese Lore
appearance: Yaoguai can appear in a multitude of forms, depending on their origin and current state. They can take the shape of their original animal or plant form, a human form (often with telltale signs of their true nature, such as animalistic features), or a monstrous hybrid form. Their appearance often reflects their personality and power, with more powerful Yaoguai exhibiting more elaborate and imposing forms. They may possess glowing eyes, unnatural skin colors, or other otherworldly features.
habitat: Yaoguai can inhabit a wide range of environments, from remote mountains and deep forests to ancient temples and bustling cities. They often choose locations that are rich in spiritual energy or that hold significance to their personal history. Some Yaoguai prefer to live in seclusion, while others actively seek out interactions with humans or other supernatural beings.
behavior: The behavior of Yaoguai is highly variable, depending on their nature, motivations, and experiences. Some Yaoguai are benevolent and seek to help humans, while others are malevolent and delight in causing chaos and destruction. Many Yaoguai are simply mischievous and enjoy playing tricks on unsuspecting mortals. Their behavior is often influenced by their past experiences and their interactions with the world around them.

keyMyths.title: Journey to the West
keyMyths.description: Many Yaoguai appear as antagonists in Journey to the West, testing the strength and virtue of the pilgrims. These Yaoguai often seek to consume Tang Sanzang to gain immortality.

keyMyths.title: The Legend of the White Snake
keyMyths.description: Bai Suzhen, a white snake Yaoguai, falls in love with a human and faces opposition from a Buddhist monk who seeks to destroy her. This story explores themes of love, prejudice, and the conflict between the human and supernatural realms.
keyMyths.source: The Legend of the White Snake

sources.author: Anthony C. Yu
sources.description: English translation and annotation of the classic Chinese novel.
sources.text: English translation and annotation of the classic Chinese novel.

sources.author: Various Retellings
